text: Members of the solute carrier organic anion transporting polypeptide (OATPs) family of proteins have been identified as the transporters responsible for the disposition of a wide range of amphipathic organic anions, which include a number of endogenous compounds as well as a large number of drugs, including bile salts and their conjugates, steroids, thyroid hormones, statins, antibiotics, antivirals, as well as anticancer drugs. OATP1B1 is primarily responsible for the hepatic uptake of such compounds and is therefore a key determinant of their disposition. Accordingly, both the FDA and the EMA have recommended that assessment of drug-drug interactions (DDI) involving OATP1B1 should be performed in vitro. Unfortunately, the structure and transport mechanism of the OATPs have been poorly defined. Here, we report the cryoEM structures of the human OATP1B1 protein in complex with representative endogenous substrates (i.e., bilirubin, E3S), a clinical drug (i.e., simeprevir), and a fluorescent compound (i.e., 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein), both in the outward-open and inward-open conformations. These structures delineate the major and minor substrate binding pockets as well as the conformational changes that occur during the transport cycle. Combined with a number of mutagenesis studies and MD simulations, our data provide new insights into the mechanism of OATP1B1-mediated transport, and serve as a structural platform for modeling and predicting potential drug-drug interactions involving OATP1B1 and other members of the OATP family.
